column1: # Principle: The four-form mirror equivalence rule for 2-arg words.
# Per CLAUDE.md (Argument Ordering, post-§1.4): every Signature has
# a BarrierPos. For a 2-arg signature with the default barrier (= N),
# all four physical arrangements of word + args produce the SAME sig
# binding, EXCEPT one — the so-called "swap form".
# Given a non-commutative 2-arg word `f` with handler that computes
# `args[1] - args[0]`, the canonical four forms are:
#   f a b    — all-forward                : sig=[a,b]
#   b f a    — 1 prefix + 1 forward (post)  : sig=[a,b]    (mirror of all-forward)
#   b a f    — all-prefix                  : sig=[a,b]    (mirror)
#   a f b    — 1 prefix + 1 forward (pre)   : sig=[b,a]    (SWAP — the only odd one)
# Three of the four forms are equivalent; the fourth — the swap form
# — is the only arrangement where the prefix value lands at sig[1]
# instead of sig[0]. This is intentional: it lets the source `10
# sub 3` read left-to-right as "10 minus 3", matching how a human
# parser scans the line.
# This file uses the spec runner's `sub` word (registered with handler
# `args[0] - args[1]`) so the four expected outcomes are -7, -7, -7, +7
# for the four forms applied to (10, 3). A clean four-row table.
# Why this matters
# The mirror rule is the cleanest user-visible knob in the dispatch
# system. If it ever broke (e.g. a refactor of matchSignature changed
# the stack-walk direction or rearrangeForForward shifted a slot),
# every infix-style call site in the language would silently swap.
# This is precisely the kind of regression a test row should catch
# at PR time, not after a release. CLAUDE.md describes the rule in
# prose — these rows lock the prose into executable form.
# Algebraic note: the rule is sometimes phrased as "left-to-right
# nearest-first": forward-collected args fill sig from index 0
# upward in source order; remaining sig slots fill from the stack
# top downward. The four forms above just enumerate the (forward,
# prefix) splits for arity 2. See multiarg.tsv for the arity-3
# extension.
# tokens
sub 10 3
column3: form: f a b      → forward=[a=10,b=3], sig=[10,3], 10-3 = 7

column1: 3 sub 10
column3: form: b f a      → forward=[a=10],     stk top=b=3,    sig=[10,3], 10-3 = 7

column1: 3 10 sub
column3: form: b a f      → forward=[],         stk top=a=10 deeper=b=3, sig=[10,3], 10-3 = 7

column1: 10 sub 3
column3: form: a f b      → SWAP: forward=[b=3], stk top=a=10, sig=[3,10], 3-10 = -7

column1: # Cross-check with `concat` (also forward-prec, also non-commutative).
# Handler is plain string concatenation (args[0] + args[1]); inputs
# chosen so source-order vs swapped is visually obvious.
concat "ab" "cd"
cd concat "ab"
cd "ab" concat
column3: mirror: all-prefix; stack top=ab, deeper=cd, sig=[ab,cd]

column1: ab concat "cd"
